sentences.text: Yes.
You know, one would think that with the physical liberation, this would more or less be the end of all the traumatic feelings, and all the anxieties, and all the worries.
Unfortunately, based on my own life experience, the-- this issue and, and, and its effect on people didn't end with liberation.
I don't think it has even ended today, because the need for us to do this interview itself shows that we still have-- dealing with a big, big problem here.

And unfortunately, based on my perception of what's going on in this world, I think it will never, ever totally dissolve itself or go away as, as, as, as a crushing burden on whoever has a Jewish conscience.

Anyway, the incident that, that kind of led into the post-liberation-- I say not post-war, post-liberation because we were liberated in the fall because we were-- but other parts-- the war wasn't over.
The war was only over, I would say, approximately six, seven months after we were liberated.
And the-- And so, so, so there's a difference.
Also, right after liberation, our liberation, there was the Battle of the Bulge.
And I remember very well-- I'm sorry.
I know you asked about the incident in the park, but this is all part of our-- of what-- of, of, our-- our, our environment, the psychological make up right after the war.

Because what happened was that [NON-ENGLISH] was given the task of doing an offensive, a counter-offensive, in the Belgian Ardennes in the winter of 1944 to try to change the tide of war, and also to give the, the Germans more time to develop their secret weapons, which were these, these, these, these-- the, the, the, the forerunners of the Scuds, you know, the flying missiles, the V-1s and V-2s, which were targeted at Antwerp and at London, England.
To give them more time, and they thought they were going to come up with some other secret weapons, such as-- they were working heavy water.
They were working, as we found out later, even on nuclear weapons.
So to buy time.
And all of a sudden because of the winter and bad visibility, and the American Air Force didn't have a-- couldn't, couldn't penetrate.
And the two were locked behind us.
And then the episode with Skorzeny, as we know, with-- donning America uniforms and infiltrating.
So they were quite successful.
They were coming back and in our direction.
And we were nearby.
So there was a new panic there that, hey, the Germans are coming back.
It's not over.
They're going to finish off what they-- what they couldn't finish.
We thought that they were going to come back and occupy us again.
It's only when-- it's only the-- when Patton threw the counter, counter offensive and they changed the Battle of the Bulge.
This was one anxiety.
The next thing was, that after the city was liberated, Antwerp, which is not often talked about, was also targeted as one of the, for the flying bombs, the V-1s, like London.
Because they knew that Antwerp was going to be a big supply harbor for the Allied war effort.
So we were living in a city where on-going, during the day, bombs would, would explode.
There used to be two types, the V-1s and the V-2s.
The V-1s were the smaller ones.
There was an engine.
There was a flame.
You could see it when the engine stopped.
The bomb would fall and it would destroy the equivalent of a bungalow.
The V-2s were faster than the speed of sound.
There was no warning.
It would-- it could destroy a whole city block.
Now this had nothing to do with us being Jewish.
This was for the population at large.
But it's still part of our experience.
Oh, I witnessed those.
I mean, I recall that we, we had no money.
The place where we had lived before the war had been taken over by others.
And although some friends of my, my father wanted to help him to rebuild his business, his salon, that took capital.
Unfortunately, the Belgian government and the finance minister Gutt, G-U-T-T, Gutt, decided, like in many other countries, to devaluate the franc, not-- to, to, to fight rampant inflation.
So there could not be more than 2,000 francs in circulation.
So if I-- I had no money, could not exchange-- or had no possessions, like land, or businesses, or houses.
There was no cash, so you had nothing.
So there was no way to start a business because there was no cash.
So we were living-- my father had to make a living.
He couldn't do his own line of work.

He befriended some Jewish Allied soldiers because any one who was-- who was Jewish in the Canadian Armed Forces, the American Armed Forces, the British forces, and even the Palestinian Brigade, my father was like open door.
I mean, whatever he-- he used to, to feast those people, to honor them, to celebrate them.
And they also did some business with him.
He managed to get some, some, some highly desirable goods like, like, alcohol, cigarettes, and chocolates, and used to get them as gifts or at a cheap price.
And I used to go with my dad with heavy suitcases to the, to the countryside, to the inns, inns and cafes and parks.
And we had that little black market going in contraband and, and smuggling this, this stuff through.
So, so, as a matter of fact, the experience we-- we were living, as I said, in orange crates.
And we had practically no money.
Every Friday night, my mother used to give us five francs, my brother and I, to go to the swimming club, which was about 10 minutes walk from home.
The Astrid Batt it was called.
Astrid Swimming Club in the [NON-ENGLISH] to go and swim and take a shower to be cleansed.

That one particular Friday, that, that week that opened up in the Rex Theater on the main street of Antwerp on the De Keyserlei, the movie, which was a 1939 beginning of the war movie, or middle of the war movie.
And for the Europeans to have after all this Nazi propaganda was something unreal.
So I was dying to go and see that movie.
It was also five francs.
So I said to my mother, Mama, please could we go to the movie?
I was dying to see this movie, All the kids are talking about She says no, I only have five francs.
Be a good boy and go.
Don't go to the movie.
Take your brother and go to the swimming club.
So I listened to my mother.
And if had I gone to the-- to the-- to the movie instead, I wouldn't be sitting here.
Because while we were in the swimming club, the performance we were going to see in the movie, a V-2 bomb hit the Rex Theater and killed 1,500 people.
One person survived.

And that was the movie I was going to see, exactly that Friday night, instead of going to the-- just, just to talk about-- as a matter of fact, on the way to that swimming club, I passed, I come in front of this house, and a wonderful smell comes out of the basement.
And I look and it's a field kitchen of the Canadian Air Force, and they're doing French toast.
And so the smoke would come out of the basement.
I hadn't had a piece of white bread for, for, for years.
And I stare and I stare.
I couldn't get away from that smell of the French toast.
So the guy, the cook, looks at me and he says, what is it?
I says, well, I said, I look-- I says, I'm sorry.
I don't know what language I spoke.
Maybe I spoke a few words of English because we had a few words of English in that private school.
And I was always good in languages, so I picked up very quickly.
And I looked and he says, would like some?
Would you like some?
I give him my towel and he puts in half a loaf of white bread.
Didn't know that white bread wasn't healthy.
White bread was the highest of luxury.
And I come with this half loaf of white bread and I put it on the table at home.
And my father, my parents were astonished.
Where did you get this from?
So I explained that it was this, this kitchen.
So I said, the kitchen.
So they said, do you think you could get some food there?
So I go back the next morning and I-- and I start talking to this cook.
And I said, look.
I says, our family, we don't have a lot to eat.
We are very hungry.
And, and is there anyway that I can buy some food from you?
He looks at me.
And he took a liking to me.
And says, OK.
If you come very early before anybody gets up, he says, like 5:00, 5:30, when we-- when I open this kitchen, and you bring me 50 francs, which was like $1.00, he says he I'll load you up with--
So here I am.
It's pitch black.
It's the winter.
All-- there's no lights in the city.
There's no lights, like you have lights in the street, because everybody was afraid of the air raids.
And the flying bombs are going over.
And I'm with my bag.
And I was--
[LAUGHS]
How old am I? 11 years old.
And I go there to this kitchen, and the guy starts loading me up with corned beef, and cigarettes, and-- I mean, a whole supply.
And I kept-- and this is-- and I went going there once a week.
Now, when I-- so I carried this bag.
It's pitch dark.
All of a sudden, I see a flying bomb go over.
This is the one you could see, with the, with the taillight.
And it stops, so I hide.
So I, I go into a porch and hide and hope and pray it's going to fly over me before the engine stops.
But I went every morning, pitch dark.
And this fed our family for a whole winter.
Ironically, after a while, my parents, my family started getting a little bit bored with the-- with what I was bringing.
Again this?
And again--
[LAUGHS]
It was enough that I-- that I-- that's human nature, you know?
[LAUGHTER] Anyway, so this is the experience.
And bombs were just, bombs in the cities just went up, and, and, and, just were destroyed like that.
This-- that took several months.
Now, but the episode in the park, which is another episode which-- some things happen in your life that shape your life, that leave a lasting impression and make, and make a big difference.
It's all related.
And as I said before, the, the, the, the persecution and the trauma didn't stop with the liberation.
One day, on-- it's in the winter of 1944.
We are liberated and living in the condition I just described.
And it's a very, very cold winter.
The park is frozen solid.
There, there, there was a pond there, frozen solid.
So solid that you could go and, and slide.
There's no such thing as-- so, and people used to-- kids used to slide.
They used to make a little track, used to run, and then slide on the-- on-- squatting.
And I'm going there.
And I'm a very, very weak and skinny little kid because of this [INAUDIBLE] treatment.
But I go and watch the other kids slide.
And I'm standing there, and all of a sudden, I hear this conversation.
And there's this big bully of a kid, must have been-- a giant compared to myself, probably three, four years older.
And he's talking to this bunch of little boys, about a dozen little boys, and telling them about the Jews that are coming back from wherever they are.
And that they should put all the Jews that are coming back on a ship-- exactly like that, in Flemish-- and take that ship to the middle of the sea, the North Sea, and sink the ship.
That's what he told these little boys.
He probably lived in a house that had been-- belonged to Jewish people, because it was the neighborhood where-- the middle of-- where the religious Jewish people lived, the observant ones.
Because I always make a distinction between religious and observant.
These are two different terms.
And they have to evacuate, and they-- there were resentful and probably said that, and the kid heard it.
And I hear that.
And I was so keenly aware of everything that had been going on, that most of my family were gone, that there had been this destruction, my own suffering, my family, my parents.
And I-- and I--and I hear this kid say that.
And it was like, like, like, like putting a knife through my heart.
And I just, I can't leave, I cannot just walk away from this.
My dad, and the tradition in our family is not to walk away from, from, from, from issues like that.
I don't know what to do.
I can't go and jump 15 little kids, especially one big bully.
So I wait, and wait, and wait.
And finally, they're finished, and they go home.
And lo and behold, the bully that said those horrible words goes along home by himself.
And I follow him.
And he walks towards the major street that had been the major Jewish street in the Jewish district, in the area where we had been taking those letters.
And passed that area where I had seen that truck and those Germans pick up the people on the street.
And he turns the corner into the [NON-ENGLISH].
Any Jew from Antwerp or people who have to know the [NON-ENGLISH] is that-- is the street that was a Jewish area.
I was going to say, almost like here, Battery Street.
[LAUGHS] And he-- and he walks in.
And he's halfway on the block.
And I jump this kid with every possible rage that I had in me.
And I jump this kid and I start pummelling him, and kicking him, and beating him up.
Now, if he had just turned around and given me one swat, he would have just-- I was just-- but he didn't know what hit him.
And he got-- and he was really-- and he started to sob and cry.
And I was-- my feud-- he finally got out from under and he, and he took off.
And he-- I felt very, very good.
Very good that I hadn't, had faced it.
So that was this experience.
And the reason I, I, I bring this up was that we were just liberated, and you have to hear this again.
Now, the antisemitism, race hatred, you name it, even to this day, all over the world, has been going on ever since.
The issue has not gone away in the minds of the people.
But it started even right then, right after the liberation.
Now, what happened was that, as I said-- by the way, my father escaped from this train to Auschwitz.
He died on January 16, 1971, after I had just moved from Montreal to Toronto.
And he died-- he was a very fit person.
He had always been a sportsman.
He was a very good long-distance swimmer, as a matter of fact.
And he used to only associate with younger people.
He used to go to the swimming club on a regular basis.
And one day they come out of the swimming club on that day, the day after my mother's birthday, and they stand on the edge of this boulevard.
And they're waiting to cross the street, and a car gets out of control with a drunken driver and hits-- picks up my father between the other two people, and drives into a tree.
And three hours later, my father is dead.
So he died in peace time, in good health, on the sidewalk after escaping a train to, to to Auschwitz.
So my mother then was left alone.
And eventually, she ended up in a, in a, in a senior home because-- I don't want-- the details, there's too many details here-- in a senior home in Antwerp, Belgium.
Same town.
Now, I used to visit regularly.
I used to bring her over and, and go to places with her.
I always had a very close relationship with her.
In 1994, she couldn't travel anymore.
So I have my son David here, who, by the way, is ultra, ultra orthodox.
And he had two children at the time.
So since she couldn't come here, I took him over.
I sent him over with the two children to visit my mother.
The following year, the third little great-grandson was born, Avraham Dov.
My mother had never-- no, sorry.
Yes, yes.
I went there, because now we've got a fourth one.
My mother had never seen him, and she couldn't travel.
So I took the whole family.
My son, the religious one, his wife, and the three kids, to visit my mother in the senior home.
Well, my brother, being very orthodox-- my son, I mean-- being very orthodox.
As a matter of fact, he, on Shabbat, he wears a schtreimel and a kittel and all this, so you can imagine.
And now we're in this town of Antwerp.
And I had rented a hotel suite where he could be near kosher food shops and restaurants and so forth.
It had to be ultra kosher for him.
So, so, so, it's .
Shabbat, it's Saturday.
And I'm walking from my mother's senior home with my mother in a wheelchair.
My brother in total-- Your son.
My son.
I keep saying my brother.
My son in his regalia, Shabbat, regalia, in a white kittel and a schtreimel.
And then the three kids and my wife and I.
And we, we were walking exactly the same route that, on the same sidewalk, in the same spot where, where, 50 years earlier, 52 years earlier, I had seen them rounding up these Jews.
I want to turn back.
So I am very conscious.

And not only that, but the, the, the local Gentile population, whatever they see-- look-- people who look like my brother, or my son-- I don't know why I keep saying my brother-- like my son, he-- they start looking.
They start laughing, they start poking, they start watching.
They start pointing.
So I'm very conscious of that.
And, and, and because of what happened, I am very on edge.
And I was always afraid that somebody was going to jump him, or attack him, or insult him.
He knows from nothing.
He has no fear.
He's oblivious.
And I'm walking with these memories from, from 50 years before, exactly in those worst spots.
And it did affect-- it did affect my feeling.
I was totally afraid.
I was watching around.
I was just waiting for someone to do something and for me to pounce.
Now, the point I was trying to make is-- and talk about coincidences-- and by the way, I told my son and the, and the children what happened here, and what happened on the pond and so forth.

And it so happens, and this is almost unbelievable as a coincidence, that the place where my son and his kids stayed in 1994, which, by the way, this was the last time my mother saw them because she passed away six months later.
So it was a good thing that we went on that trip or she would never have seen the latest great grandson at the time.

But the address, that the apartment building attached to the hotel where my son, where my son stayed, that front door was exactly, exactly the same spot where in '44, 50 years earlier I had jumped that bully, the antisemitic bully, that had-- that, that, that had actually talked about sinking all the Jews in the sea.
That had to be my own son and their great grandchildren, grandchildren, would be exact in that same spot in a city of a million people.
You can imagine the emotional feelings that I went through.
It was-- it was-- it was very traumatic.
And it's very hard to explain to people that haven't gone-- you know, 50 years later to be in exactly the same area.
And being then so self-conscious about his, his, his obvious conspicuous appearance.

Because it always ran through my mind that if he had, he had been alive then, if I'd been like that and raised like that, I wouldn't be alive today because there was no way you could escape the, the, the, the, the Holocaust if you were looking like that.
And that always goes through my mind, even now.
I don't mind him being ultra-Orthodox.
I don't mind him observing everything, 100%.
But I have very, very great strong feelings of anxiety about his, about his, about his, his appearance in, in, in a hostile world because what it could lead to.
That's not rational, but that' just part of the after effect of what happened, you see?
Anyway.
You told me about another incident that happened outside the Toronto City Hall when there was a demonstration.
Yes.
Yes, about the-- oh, just a few years ago in Toronto.
Because when we talk about how do I feel about the world and the issue, all-- so many years later, I'm, I'm an optimist, in terms of my initiatives.
I, I like to be a good father.
I was a good son to my mother.
I like to be a good husband.
I like to do all-- keep things in balance, treat all my fellow men well.
But when-- but when I see proliferation of, of, of swastikas and, and, and hate mongers and, and, and [INAUDIBLE] the [INAUDIBLE] and the [INAUDIBLE] and the-- and all these other people.

The fact that even though, we have this, this, this uproar about this, this, this Latvian S.S killer living peacefully as a refugee in, in, in an apartment building where a lot of Holocaust survivors live who are totally disturbed.

When I see all that and see what's going with Israel, and, and Islamic antisemitism, and the Farrakhans and all this, I-- and also, the fact that there is not enough outrage based on what happened in World War II to make this like, unthinkable, impossible, then I get very disturbed.
And I-- and, and, and I get very, very cynical.

It has to do-- it has to do with the lack of-- lack-- the indifference has to do-- this indifference, this lack of awareness, lack of knowledge, and lack of care, by a lot of Jewish people as well, not only by non-Jewish people.

For example, as I mentioned, there was this episode where Chaim Herzog, President of Israel, visited Canada and addressed an audience at the Sheraton Hotel, just on Queen Street at Nathan Phillips Square.
When I heard, we went there.
We parked our car underneath and went to attend.
In the middle of his speech, news filtered in that there were demonstrations outside.
And he said, there are some-- there's some Palestinians demonstrating with all kinds of flags and, and, and symbols and slogans.
And he said, they'll probably-- the the reporters, he said, would probably give more importance to that news than to what I have to say.
So I was very curious.
So my wife and I stepped outside to see what was going on.
And the scene was that across from the hotel, cordoned off, because there were police, was this group.
Maybe a couple hundred Palestinians with Palestinian flags.
And they had a little girl that was holding a, a, a Jewish flag, a Jewish Israeli flag, with the Star of David, like [INAUDIBLE].
And inside the Magen David, there was a swastika.
Talk about, about, about instigation, and talk about, about, about insulting.
Talk about obscenity.
Around me were all kinds of groups.
And on my left, there was a group of Jewish youngsters that was singing and dancing, Zionist, the hora.
There was a group of people who I knew were survivors of the Holocaust.
As a matter of fact, there was even another group on the right with a banner, Justice for [NON-ENGLISH], who was then being tried in Israel.
So I see this swastika in this Star of David, in this Magen David, in the shield of David.
And I just can't stand there, like I couldn't stand at the pond and let it go away.
So what do I do?
I'm still dressed in my office suit.
I walk across the square, and I grabbed the, the flag and tear it out of the child's hands.
The moment I do that, in one shot, about 20, 30 people, Palestinians, jump me.
They start tearing at me and beating at me.
And not a single person around me, on the other side, came in, joined, lifted a finger, or did anything.
The police grabs me and arrests me for disturbing the peace.
So they drag me down to the basement, security office of the Sheraton Hotel, keep me there for an evening.
Asked my wife, says, what, what, what are you doing there with my husband?
As a matter I still have a shirt that was stored, hanging as a trophy here for having walked away from this thing, hanging in my cupboard.
And the irony was that the policeman could not understand why I was upset.
They didn't understand why the swastika was so provocative.
My wife says, why did they-- why are you keeping my husband?
They says, well, because he says there's no room for swastikas in Canada.
He asked me if I'm normal, if I'm excited, if I have disturbances.
I says, no.
I'm perfectly normal.
I know exactly what I'm doing.
But I says, but I think you people don't know what, what this is all about.
So one of them stayed with me downstairs.
As a matter fact, I went-- I told him, sit down.
Relax.
We can talk.
He says, no.
I'm not supposed to sit down because I have a prisoner.
I'm supposed to stand.
I says, can, can I go and tell my wife what, what, what what's happened, that I'm OK?
No, no.
You can't go.
So they send someone else.
And I says-- and he couldn't understand why I was upset.
Now, that's the message.
How can a policeman that are assigned to watch over an Israeli president of a Jewish people, our people there, not understand what a swastika is all about?
In Germany, the swastika is forbidden.
You-- it is against the law to show the swastika, as it is against the law to deny the Holocaust.
That's the law in Germany.
Here, you are allowed to, to, to show the swastika.

So the two things in an incident, which you asked me to remember was, the ignorance of the-- of even the authorities, about this whole episode, because they don't even know why a swastika should be an irritant to, to anybody, and to Jewish people, especially Jewish people who survived the Holocaust.
And that there was not enough solidarity and indignation among the people around me to come and join me and lend a helping hand.
That's part of my--
OK.
I came forward because-- I, I, I can think of, of three reasons.
And I would say that the, the catalyst-- because I have actually not joined, so far, into survive-- with other people to testify.
I have not joined the, the, the Hidden Children Movement and so forth.
Because I hadn't heard or seen a situation whereby the-- this ironic twist being maltreated so badly, that's redundant, being maltreated by the people who are supposed to hide us from the oppressor.
And that having one of your oppressors be so kind to you, that twist I hadn't heard and seen.
I, I, I had, I had difficulty with talking about these people who, after all, did save our lives.
But I thought that an initiative like Mr.

Spielberg's to-- for the record-- to immortalize what happened, I think deserves the support of anyone who has anything to contribute, because he's actually writing history as factually as it can be written by first-hand testimony from people, and not, and not, the kind of revisionism that is written by most-- most histories are-- of most historic episode.

Plus, the fact that I wanted to, at least, be on-- have on the record that not all the people that hid Jewish children were nice people, did it out of the goodness of their hearts, that some of them could be-- could do it for, for pecuniary reasons, for the advantage of improving, for the money, for a better standard of living.
And it could be still sadistic and bad.
And that in all this blackness, and all this-- and all this horror, I like to talk about the flower in the desert.
They could be among the enemy.
You're supposed to be in [INAUDIBLE], still a human being.
Come out and teach, and tell the people that's supposed to be the patriots, yes, I want them on the picture.
[HEBREW] Human beings.
So that this man, sure.
I cannot forgive the Nazis for what they did.
But I've also learned the lesson that not all the people who are supposed to be good, can be good.
And not all the people who are supposed to be bad are going to be bad.
And we should only judge people on their own merit because they can't control their behavior towards other, towards their fellow men.
But they can't control where they come from or what their origin is.
